does: This paper introduces REMEDI, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating machine unlearning methods specifically in clinical disease inference tasks. The benchmark addresses multi-label and multiclass disease prediction problems where models must unlearn patient-specific data while maintaining performance on retained data. REMEDI is constructed from real clinical datasets with characteristics that make unlearning particularly challenging, including correlated disease labels, longitudinal patient records, and safety constraints inherent to medical applications. The paper establishes evaluation metrics for assessing both unlearning completeness and retention quality, providing a realistic testbed that goes beyond synthetic datasets used in prior unlearning benchmarks.
connects: This work builds on the intersection of machine unlearning literature and clinical NLP. It extends prior machine unlearning methods developed primarily for non-medical domains to the specific constraints of healthcare, where privacy regulations like HIPAA and GDPR create practical demands for data removal from trained models. The paper responds to limitations in existing unlearning benchmarks, which typically rely on synthetic data that fails to capture the structural complexity of real medical records. It connects to clinical NLP work on disease inference while addressing the gap between general-purpose unlearning techniques and their applicability to medical language models with unique characteristics like label correlations and temporal dependencies in patient histories.
enables: REMEDI enables development and systematic evaluation of unlearning methods tailored for medical AI systems, supporting future work on privacy-preserving clinical models. The benchmark facilitates research into unlearning approaches that can handle complex medical data structures while maintaining model safety and clinical utility. This work opens directions for studying the trade-offs between unlearning effectiveness and retention performance in high-stakes medical domains, potentially leading to certified unlearning methods that satisfy regulatory requirements. It also enables investigation of how unlearning affects inter-label relationships and temporal patterns in disease models, supporting broader efforts toward responsible and compliant deployment of clinical AI systems.
